metadata.summary: Run an agent that proposes invariants (round-trip, idempotence, oracle-equivalence, metamorphic, monotonicity) and writes them as property-based tests before example-based tests.

content.title: Property-Based Tests From LLM-Inferred Invariants
content.summary: For any pure function with a docstring, type signature, or one example call, run an agent that proposes invariants — round-trip, idempotence, oracle-equivalence, metamorphic, monotonicity — and writes them as @given Hypothesis (Python), fast-check (TS), or proptest (Rust) tests before writing example-based tests. The agent must justify each property from the source text and reject any property it cannot ground there. Anthropic's own red-team agent ran this loop on 100 popular Python packages (NumPy, SciPy, Pandas) and produced 984 bug reports across 786 modules with 56% true-bug rate on a manual sample (86% on top-ranked).

why.p: LLMs are demonstrably better at inferring algebraic properties from a docstring than at inventing example-based tests — examples drift toward the implementation, properties drift toward the spec. Property-based runners then shrink failures to minimal counter-examples, producing a debug-friendly bug report instead of a long log of one-off cases. Combining the two — agent proposes properties, runner shrinks failures — closes a gap example-based tests structurally cannot: discovering edge cases the developer never considered.

list.item: Pure logic: serialization, parsing, math, sorting, dedup, schema validators, anything with an inverse or algebraic property.
Code with a clear oracle (a slow reference implementation, a previous version, a spec equivalent).
Stateless API endpoints whose request/response can be modelled as types.
After example tests pass — properties find the cases the developer did not write.
Stateful side-effecting code without clear invariants (use stateful PBT only with care; see Hypothesis RuleBasedStateMachine).
UI rendering / "looks right" code — there is no algebraic property.
Code where the spec is "whatever the current output is" — properties become tautologies.
Tight CI windows where shrinking time exceeds the budget — gate properties on a nightly job instead.

section.name: property-rubric
section.p: An open prompt ("write properties for this function") yields generic invariants the runner cannot shrink usefully. A closed rubric forces the agent to map the function to one of five named patterns — each with a known shape, easy to read, easy to review.

rule.statement: The agent MUST classify each proposed property under exactly one of: `round-trip` (`f(g(x)) == x`), `idempotence` (`f(f(x)) == f(x)`), `oracle-equivalence` (`f(x) == oracle(x)`), `metamorphic` (`f(t(x))` relates to `f(x)` by some `R`), or `invariant-on-output` (`P(f(x))` holds for all valid `x`). Any property the agent cannot label MUST be rejected and not committed.
rule.rationale: A closed rubric (a) makes review tractable, (b) forces the agent to think about the function's algebraic structure rather than its current implementation, (c) matches the categories Hypothesis/fast-check/proptest already shrink against well.

example.note: Each test names its class; reviewer sees the shape immediately.
Not a property — only checks the runner returns *something*.

antipattern.description: Agent emits a "property" that asserts the same thing as an example test on random data.
antipattern.reason: Without an algebraic relationship, random inputs add runtime cost without finding edge cases.

section.name: source-grounded-rule
section.p: An ungrounded property reflects what the agent thinks the function should do, not what the spec says. Grounding each property in a quoted docstring fragment, a type signature, or an example call turns "agent confidently invents wrong invariants" (the most common failure mode at >100k corpora) into "agent rejects properties it cannot justify" — exactly the behaviour the Anthropic study isolated as the difference between 56% and 86% true-bug rate.

rule.statement: Every committed property MUST carry a one-line comment of the form `# grounded: <quoted source fragment>` immediately above the `@given` (or `fc.assert` / `proptest!`) decorator. Properties with no quotable source MUST be deleted, not weakened. Code review MUST reject any property whose grounding comment does not appear in the source under test.
rule.rationale: The grounding comment makes review O(seconds) per property and turns the "specification gap" into a hard error: if the agent cannot quote a source line, the spec is silent on the invariant and the property is the agent's hallucination, not the function's contract.

example.note: Comment quotes the docstring; reviewer can grep the source.

antipattern.description: Agent fixes a failing property by weakening the assertion until it passes.
antipattern.reason: Weakening turns a real bug report into a tautology; the bug remains, the test goes silent.

template.purpose: Prompt fragment listing the 5 property classes and source-grounding rule.
Hypothesis test skeleton with the 5 canonical property classes wired up.
